title: Big Countries Meet to Talk About Oil Prices
summary: The G7, a group of seven big countries, is having an emergency meeting on Monday because oil prices are going up fast. Oil is now over $100 a barrel, and stock markets are falling. This is happening because there is a war between the US, Israel, and Iran. The war is causing problems in the Strait of Hormuz, a key place where oil ships pass through. About one-fifth of the world's oil usually goes through there, but now it has almost stopped. This makes oil prices rise, which can make things more expensive for people and businesses. The G7 might release oil from their reserves to help lower prices. Over the weekend, there were more attacks in Iran and nearby countries, which scared the markets. Oil prices jumped by 25% in Asia on Monday before going down a bit.

keywords.explanation: a group of seven big countries that work together on economic issues
the cost of oil, which is used for energy and making things
a narrow waterway where many oil ships travel
a special meeting called quickly to deal with a problem
places where people buy and sell shares of companies
stored oil that countries keep for emergencies

background_read: The G7 is a group of countries including the US, UK, and others that meet to talk about big world problems. Oil is a natural resource used for energy, like in cars and factories. When there is a war or trouble in places where oil comes from, it can make oil prices go up, which affects everyone by making things more expensive. Stock markets show how well companies are doing, and when people are worried, stock prices can fall.
Article_Structure: WHO: The G7 nations, including finance ministers like UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves
WHAT: Holding an emergency meeting to discuss surging oil prices and stock market slumps
WHERE: In the Strait of Hormuz and globally, affecting markets worldwide
WHY: Because of the US-Israeli war with Iran disrupting oil supplies and causing economic concerns

perspectives.description: The war is making oil prices rise, which can hurt businesses and consumers by increasing costs.
